items.q: In Pavlov's experiment, what is the conditioned response?
items.correct: Salivation to the bell alone, after pairing with food.
items.misconception: Salivation to the food (which is automatic).
items.random_wrong: The dog ringing the bell itself.
items.why: Students confuse the unconditioned response (automatic salivation to food) with the conditioned response (learned salivation to bell). The conditioned response is the LEARNED one.
Students reverse IV and DV. The IV is what you CHANGE (input); the DV is what you MEASURE (outcome). Mnemonic: 'I change the I.'

items.q: How are phobias MAINTAINED according to Mowrer's two-process model?
items.correct: Through operant conditioning - avoidance reduces anxiety (negative reinforcement).
items.misconception: Through classical conditioning continuing over time.
items.why: Classical conditioning ACQUIRES the phobia; operant conditioning MAINTAINS it. Students conflate these. Avoiding the feared object reduces anxiety, reinforcing the avoidance.

items.q: SSRIs are typically used to treat:
items.why: Correct answer: Depression. “Schizophrenia” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Systematic desensitisation is mainly used for:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Phobias. “Depression” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many main therapy approaches are taught at GCSE here?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
